name: Roentgenium
name_origin: Named after German physicist Wilhelm Conrad Rontgen, the discoverer of X-rays and the 1901 Nobel Prize laureate in Physics.

discovery.discoverer: Gesellschaft fur Schwerionenforschung (GSI) team led by Sigurd Hofmann

occurrence.abundance_summary: Roentgenium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via cold-fusion reactions such as 64Ni + 209Bi.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of roentgenium has been experimentally characterized. By analogy with its lighter group 11 homologs (copper, silver, gold) it is predicted to be a noble metal, with relativistic calculations suggesting an unusually stable +3 oxidation state and possible noble-gas-like inertness in some configurations. The listed configuration [Rn] 5f14 6d9 7s2 is the periodic-trend prediction; relativistic models also suggest [Rn] 5f14 6d10 7s1 (gold-like) is plausible.
isotopes_summary: All roentgenium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 282Rg with a half-life of about 100 seconds, identified as a decay product in chains from heavier elements. Other isotopes have half-lives ranging from milliseconds to tens of seconds.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Roentgenium
